metadata.title: Causal Inference — Regime-Aware Anomaly Detection
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Regime stratification back-door adjustment on NASA CMAPSS FD002.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A jet engine anomaly detector fires a hundred percent of the time in global mode — and zero percent of your alerts are real. The confounder is operating regime: high-altitude cruise and low-altitude climb look completely different in sensor space, so a model trained across all regimes flags normal climb data as failure. The fix is causal — condition on regime, block the back-door path, and let the signal speak.
beats.visual_intent: title card: global anomaly detector vs regime-conditioned detector; confounding path diagram showing op_settings -> sensors <- failure

beats.narration_text: Into the terminal: claude — build a Python anomaly detector for NASA CMAPSS FD002 data using back-door adjustment via regime stratification: KMeans with k equals six on op-settings, per-regime LinearRegression for sensors three, four, nine, eleven, and fifteen, causal z-score equals observed minus predicted over residual standard deviation, compare versus global baseline.

props.command:
claude "Build a Python anomaly detector for NASA CMAPSS FD002 data
  using back-door adjustment via regime stratification:
  KMeans(k=6) on op_settings,
  per-regime LinearRegression for sensors 3/4/9/11/15,
  causal z-score = (observed-predicted)/residual_std,
  compare vs global baseline"

beats.narration_text: Read it. The key line is the causal z-score: observed minus regime-predicted, divided by per-regime residual standard deviation. The denominator is doing the heavy lifting — each regime gets its own noise floor, so a reading that is perfectly normal in climb is not evaluated against cruise's tighter tolerance.

props.code:
# regime_anomaly.py — Claude Code output
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import numpy as np

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=6, random_state=42).fit(op_settings)
regimes = kmeans.predict(op_settings)

models = {}
for r in range(6):
    mask = regimes == r
    models[r] = LinearRegression().fit(op_settings[mask], sensors[mask])

# causal z-score: back-door adjusted
causal_z = (sensor_obs - regime_model.predict(op_settings)) / residual_std

beats.narration_text: Run it. On the left, the global baseline: alert rate a hundred percent, F1 zero — every reading is an alarm, none are real. On the right, the regime-aware detector: sixty-six percent alert rate, F1 above zero. That gap is the confounding bias the back-door adjustment removed.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: two side-by-side vertical bars comparing alert rates — GLOBAL BASELINE 100% F1=0.00 (CRIMSON) vs REGIME-AWARE 66% F1>0 (PASS_CLR green)

beats.narration_text: The alert rate is still high because sensors three and four have high natural variance in some regimes. Iterate: add sensor-level residual trimming — flag only if z-score exceeds the ninety-fifth percentile within that regime, not a fixed threshold.

props.command:
claude "update regime_anomaly.py:
  -> per-regime 95th percentile threshold
  -> flag only z > regime_p95
  -> re-run and show precision/recall"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. Precision climbs: the per-regime threshold eliminates the regime-boundary false alarms. The causal model now isolates sensor readings that are anomalous within their own operating context.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal output showing improved precision/recall after per-regime percentile thresholding

beats.narration_text: The damage was never the sensors. It was one global model applied across six mechanically distinct regimes, and the back-door path through operating conditions made everything look like an anomaly. Stratify by regime, block the back-door, and let each regime's normal define its own residual floor.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'global model -> 100% alert (all noise)' vs 'regime-stratified -> 66% (signal emerges)'; back-door path blocked diagram

beats.narration_text: Your move: pull the CMAPSS FD002 dataset and run this on your own sensor split. Experiment with k from four to eight — watch how the regime boundaries sharpen. And when your alert system fires too often, ask the first causal question: is there an operating-mode confounder I have not conditioned on?
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) try CMAPSS FD002 with your sensor split, 2) tune k for regime sharpness, 3) ask the back-door question for any alert system

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
